body_text:
Section 5.1 Cauchy's Residue Theorem
(b) This is immediate from (a) and Proposition 1(iii). Just be careful with notation as you appeal to the proposition.
(c) Since $1+z^{4}$ is nonzero inside $C$ and $\cot (\pi z)$ has simple poles at the intege it follows that $\frac{\cot (\pi z)}{1+z^{4}}$ has two simple poles inside $C$ at $z=0$ and $z=1$. Apply Theorem 1 and using (10) with $f(z)=\frac{1}{1+z^{4}}$ to compute the residues, we find
$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{C} \frac{\cot (\pi z)}{1+z^{4}} d z & =2 \pi i\left(\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{\cot (\pi z)}{1+z^{4}}, 0\right)+\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{\cot (\pi z)}{1+z^{4}}, 1\right)\right. \\
& =2 \pi i\left(\frac{1}{\pi} \frac{1}{1+0^{4}}+\frac{1}{\pi} \frac{1}{1+1^{4}}\right)=2 i\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)=3 i
\end{aligned}
$$

So far the examples that we treated involved residues at poles of fin order. There is no formula like (9) for computing the residue at an essent singularity. We have to rely on various tricks to evaluate the coefficie $a_{-1}$ in the Laurent series expansion. We illustrate with several exampl starting with one that contains a useful observation.

EXAMPLE 5 Residue at 0 of an even function
(a) Suppose that $f$ is an even analytic function with an isolated singularity at Show that
$$
\operatorname{Res}(f, 0)=0
$$
(b) Compute $\operatorname{Res}\left(e^{-\frac{1}{z^{2}}} \cos \frac{1}{z}, 0\right)$.

Solution (a) We proved in Proposition 2, Section 4.4, that if $f(z)$ is even a analytic at 0 , then the Taylor coefficients corresponding to the odd powers of $z$ zero. The same proof applies to Laurent series around 0 and shows that $f$ is even and only if $a_{2 n+1}$ and $a_{-2 n+1}$ are zero for all integers $n$. In particular, if $f$ is ev then $a_{-1}=0$, and so $\operatorname{Res}(f, 0)=0$.
(b) The function $e^{-\frac{1}{z^{2}}} \cos \frac{1}{z}$ is even and has an essential singularity at 0 . By ( its residue at 0 is 0 .

Multiplication of series is often useful in computing residues at a sing larity, including essential singularities.

EXAMPLE 6 Multiplying series by a polynomial Compute the residues of $z^{2} \sin \frac{1}{z}$ at $z=0$.
Solution From the Laurent series
$$
\sin \frac{1}{z}=\frac{1}{z}-\frac{1}{3!} \frac{1}{z^{3}}+\frac{1}{5!} \frac{1}{z^{5}}-\cdots,
$$
we get
$$
z^{2} \sin \frac{1}{z}=z-\frac{1}{3!} \frac{1}{z}+\frac{1}{5!} \frac{1}{z^{3}}-\cdots
$$
and so $\operatorname{Res}\left(z^{2} \sin \frac{1}{z}, 0\right)=-\frac{1}{3!}$.
